description: Make MK extremely token-efficient to maximize free API usage. Implement smart context pruning, fact relevance scoring, conversation history compression, and tight prompt construction. The goal: minimize tokens sent per request while maximizing response quality.
steps: Create MK_OS/orchestrator/context_builder.cpp that implements the ContextBuilder class. This class is responsible for building the smallest effective prompt for each LLM call. It takes: user input, available facts from pattern_graph, conversation history from brain_memory, tool results, and a max_tokens budget (default 1500 tokens for context). It outputs a single prompt string.

Implement fact pruning in ContextBuilder: use the existing filterRelevantFacts() logic but make it smarter. Score facts by: (a) keyword overlap with user input (existing), (b) recency of access (facts accessed recently are more relevant), (c) biographical relevance (personal facts about Mohammed always rank higher). Cap at 5 most relevant facts. Format them as terse bullet points, not full sentences.

Implement conversation history compression: instead of including the full last N turns, summarize older turns. Keep the last 2 turns verbatim. For turns 3-6, compress to a single sentence summary (e.g., 'Previously discussed: Python setup, Docker deployment'). This reduces history from ~500 tokens to ~100 tokens.

Implement a token estimator (rough: chars/4) that ensures the total prompt stays under a budget. If facts + history + system prompt exceed the budget, progressively drop: oldest history first, then lowest-scored facts, then reduce system prompt to a minimal version.

Refactor MK_SYSTEM_PROMPT to be shorter and more focused. Current prompt is ~60 words. Create a minimal version (~30 words) for when token budget is tight: 'You are MK, Mohammed's personal AI. Be concise, helpful, honest. Use provided facts to answer. Call tools when needed.'

Wire ContextBuilder into MKOrchestrator::respond() so all LLM calls go through it. Remove the old ad-hoc prompt building code (buildLLMPrompt function, the inline prompt construction in handle_natural_query).
Add tests: verify token estimator, verify fact pruning drops low-relevance facts, verify history compression produces shorter output, verify budget enforcement.
acceptance_criteria: 'make test' passes all new token efficiency tests
Prompts for simple queries use under 800 estimated tokens of context
Prompts for complex queries with many facts still stay under 2000 estimated tokens
History compression reduces 6-turn history to under 150 estimated tokens for older turns
findings: Build passes with 6 pre-existing warnings (same as before). Tests: 71 passed, 0 failed, 430 assertions. MKContextBuilder is a pure logic class (no subsystem pointers) making it easy to test. The include order in mk_entry.cpp is: context_builder.cpp -> conversation_loop.cpp (context_builder must come first since the orchestrator uses it). MK_SYSTEM_PROMPT_MINIMAL is defined as a static const in context_builder.cpp (about 20 tokens). The orchestrator's buildPrompt() now delegates to MKContextBuilder, which handles: fact scoring with personal relevance boost, history compression keeping last 2 turns verbatim, token budgeting with progressive dropping, and tool prompt optimization (only included when query seems tool-related). The existing filterRelevantFacts() at file scope in mk_entry.cpp is preserved for other code paths. Tests verify: token estimation linearity, fact scoring with keyword/personal relevance, history compression reducing size, budget enforcement under various limits, tool prompt optimization, and minimal prompt brevity. learned_facts.mk gets modified by test side effects (persistNewFact in test_tool_executor_learn_fact) - do not commit that file.
